title: Spending by international visitors while visiting a country as a share of GDP
subtitle: Money received by the destination country from foreign visitors, including spending on accommodation, food, transport, entertainment, shopping, and fares paid to the country’s own airlines, as a share of, as a share of [GDP](#dod:gdp).
note: Transactions associated with all types of foreign visitors considered, including students or seasonal workers, which can be significant in certain countries.
caption:
Spending by international visitors while visiting a country as a share of GDP

Money received by the destination country from foreign visitors, including spending on accommodation, food, transport, entertainment, shopping, and fares paid to the country’s own airlines, as a share of, as a share of GDP.

This choropleth world map displays 2024 data on international visitor spending as a share of GDP, using a color scale ranging from 0% to 5%, with intermediate thresholds at 0.5%, 1%, and 2%. The darkest teal shades, representing values at or above the 5% mark, are visible in specific nations including Croatia, Montenegro, Iceland, and Thailand. Surrounding these high-value outliers, countries such as Spain, Portugal, Greece, and Australia appear in dark teal bands that sit between the 2% and 5% range. A broad cluster of nations, including the United States, Canada, France, and China, sits in the mid-range light blue shades, generally falling between 0.5% and 1%. Large portions of South America and Africa display the lightest shades of purple or white, indicating shares near 0% or below 0.5%. Extensive areas across the African continent, along with Russia and Greenland, feature a diagonal hatching pattern denoting no available data. The visualization highlights significant variation, with the highest relative spending concentrated in specific tourism-dependent economies, while major global economies often occupy the lower to mid-range bands. Transactions associated with all types of foreign visitors are considered in this dataset, including students or seasonal workers, which can be significant in certain countries.
